text: Chapter 7 teaches the I vi ii V progression and its variant iii vi ii V, two of the most fundamental harmonic formulas in music history, typically used as song openers. The chapter organizes its extensive examples by melody and chord shape variations, requiring students to play them in sequence while paying close attention to left-hand fingering (which applies to filled noteheads, not x noteheads) and always resolving V to I. Key pedagogical principles include treating physical difficulties as dexterity training opportunities, maintaining continuity and smoothness before judging a progression's musical value, exploring the entire fingerboard from multiple angles, and using key changes (transposition) as a technique to prevent monotony. The load-bearing terminology includes the progression formula itself, the concepts of "opener," chord shapes, melody (highest pitch), and resolution, anchoring a comprehensive survey of how a single harmonic concept yields countless variations across the fingerboard.
